segments.title: When major warships were captured in combat
segments.summary: A question about captured major ships leads to the Russo-Japanese War, where battleships changed hands after combat and surrender.
segments.body: This answer distinguishes ships seized after a wider collapse from major warships captured during combat. Dr. Clarke points to the Russo-Japanese War as the clearest later example of battleships changing hands, including the Japanese acquisition of Russian vessels. He also notes that Japanese assessments of some captured Russian, French-built ships were far from enthusiastic.
segments.question: When was the last major warship captured in a war?
segments.answerShort: For major warships taken in combat, he points chiefly to the Russo-Japanese War, when Japanese forces captured Russian battleships and other vessels.

evidence.note: He contrasts World War examples with Russo-Japanese War captures and discusses captured Russian ships.

segments.title: A Napoleonic frigate repeatedly changing hands
segments.summary: A brief answer recalls a Napoleonic Wars frigate that was captured, recaptured, and taken again during the same action.
segments.body: The exchange underlines how vulnerable even commissioned warships could be to boarding, surrender, and rapid reversals during close operations. Rather than treating a prize as permanently settled, the example shows that control could change repeatedly while an action was still unfolding.
segments.question: Which ship changed hands the most times through capture and recapture?
segments.answerShort: He recalls a Napoleonic Wars frigate that was captured and recaptured repeatedly, including changes of hands within the same mission.

evidence.note: He describes a frigate in the Napoleonic Wars being captured and recaptured multiple times.

segments.title: Why King George V carried ten 14-inch guns
segments.summary: The King George V design debate turns on ambitious quadruple turrets, weight limits, rate-of-fire hopes, and the expected value of radar-controlled salvos.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that the intended three quadruple 14-inch mount arrangement became harder to fit and operate than its advocates expected. A third turret added topweight and stability problems, leaving a ten-gun compromise rather than the hoped-for twelve-gun battery. The design logic still rested on a high volume of fire, with improved loading and radar fire control expected to make 14-inch salvos dangerous even against more heavily armed opponents.
segments.question: Why did King George V use ten 14-inch guns rather than three superfiring twin turrets or a larger-calibre battery?
segments.answerShort: The design began with three quadruple 14-inch turrets, but turret reliability, topweight, and ballast problems forced a ten-gun layout; the hoped-for answer was high-rate, radar-controlled fire.

evidence.note: He explains the 14-inch quadruple-turret plan, weight problems, expected rate of fire, and radar fire-control assumptions.

segments.title: Why a fast 14-inch salvo could still be decisive
segments.summary: High shell volume changes the question from calibre alone to repeated hits, cumulative damage, and the chance of penetrating an already damaged target.
segments.body: The discussion argues that a 14-inch shell remains a serious threat once it gets through armour, and that salvo density can create that opportunity. Initial hits can damage systems or armour, while later hits may exploit the resulting weakness. It is presented as a fire-control and rate-of-fire argument, not a claim that smaller guns automatically equal larger ones in single-hit penetration.

evidence.note: He discusses 48 shells per minute, cumulative armour damage, and the practical limits of the quadruple-turret scheme.

segments.title: How R-class escorts could constrain Bismarck
segments.summary: An R-class battleship escort could make a convoy attack unattractive by forcing Bismarck to risk damage, delay, and pursuit even if she won the duel.
segments.body: The answer frames convoy escort as a strategic problem rather than a simple comparison of individual ship strength. Bismarck might defeat an R-class battleship, but a damaging fight would delay the raider, leave it exposed to stronger pursuing forces, and reduce its ability to attack the convoy. The same logic applies to other German heavy units: tactical victory can still undermine the operational mission.
segments.question: Could R-class battleships escorting convoys have constrained Bismarck?
segments.answerShort: Yes. Even if Bismarck won the engagement, an R-class could inflict damage and delay that would compromise a breakout and expose her to heavier British forces.

evidence.note: He assesses R-class convoy escorts as a deterrent because combat damage and delay would expose Bismarck to stronger forces.
